===== PAGE: Assets Documentation — Joan of the City (assets.html) =====

Assets Documentation — Joan of the City

- 

- 

- 

- 

 
 

 

 
Reference
 

## Assets Documentation
 
How every audio stem, model, silhouette, particle system, and material is named, formatted, and brought into the project — plus the visual language they all serve.
 

 

 
Assets
 

## The asset pipeline
 
Most character assets begin as T-poses and become custom silhouettes and skins — primarily for Shadow and Communion. Particle systems carry libretto text and data. Everything heavy is tracked with Git LFS so the repo stays usable.
 

 

## Start from the T-pose
Custom silhouettes & skins are built from T-poses for the Shadow and Communion presences, and for the remaining Joans as they come online (owner: Jack).
 

## Clean up & optimize textures
Texture cleanup and an optimization pass keep each scene inside its particle/texture budget — performance is a standing priority.
 

## Build particle & FX assets
Particle systems carry libretto text / data (owner: KK). Light FX (lilac sky, streaks, chromatic aberration) and Communion ripples are authored here too.
 

## Bring into Unity via LFS
Place under the right scene folder and commit on an LFS-tracked path. Confirm with git lfs status before pushing.
 

 

## Asset categories
 
 | 
 | Category | Examples | Owner
 
 
 | Custom silhouettes / skins | Shadow & Communion presences, remaining Joans | Jack
 
 | Particle / libretto systems | Particles carrying libretto text & data | KK (Kamala / Kristin)
 
 | Ravens & bird states | Flock, landing, Hero Raven animation states | Voice team
 
 | Light FX | Lilac sky, streaks, chromatic aberration layer | Nina
 
 | Audio | Scene stems & the audio cue sheet entries | Audio
 
 
 

 📦
 
Never commit a binary outside an LFS-tracked path. A raw model, texture, or stem in normal git bloats the repo permanently for everyone.
 
 

 

 
Assets
 

## Formats & budgets
 
Indicative target specs per asset type. Agreed particle and texture budgets per scene are still to be set (see Open questions) — until then, optimize aggressively and profile on-device.
 
 | 
 | Type | Format | Spec / budget
 
 
 | Score & voice stems | .wav → streamed | 48 kHz / 24-bit source; stream at runtime, don't preload a full scene.
 
 | Spatial SFX | .wav | Mono sources for positional audio; keep short clips in-memory.
 
 | Character models | .fbx / .glb | Per-scene poly budget; LODs where a character is seen at range.
 
 | Textures | .png / .tga | Power-of-two; compressed for the target device. Emissive maps for glow.
 
 | Animation | Clips on the rig | Shared humanoid rig (from the T-pose) so clips retarget across characters.
 
 
 

 

 
Assets
 

## Naming conventions
 
Predictable names make the cue sheet, search, and reviews painless. Lowercase, hyphen-separated, scoped by scene.
 
# pattern: <scene>_<category>_<name>[_<variant>]

shadow_silhouette_crawl.fbx
voice_raven_hero.fbx
voice_anim_raven-land.anim
light_particle_streak.prefab
communion_skin_01.png
shared_particle_libretto.prefab # reused across scenes
 
 | 
 | Token | Values
 
 
 | scene | light, voice, shadow, communion, real, hustle, ground, or shared
 
 | category | silhouette, skin, particle, raven, audio, tex, anim, mat
 
 | name | Descriptive, hyphen-separated (raven-land)
 
 | variant | Optional (emissive, lod1, alt)
 
 
 

 🔗
 
Asset names should match the Asset column in the cue sheet, so a cue and its asset resolve to each other with no guesswork.
 
 

 

 

 
Design
 

## Visual language
 
Each scene has its own visual language, defined by design (Deborah) and the Milanote boards. The notes below capture the signature cues per scene; the palette is a working reference, not a locked spec.

 

## Per-scene visual cues
 
 | 
 | Scene | Signature visuals
 
 
 | Light | Lilac sky growing from the speaker, streaks, a chromatic-aberration layer, audio-reactive build.
 
 | Voice | Raven flock landing in a semi-circle (illusion of occlusion), the Hero Raven, thunder-triggered collage / sky text.
 
 | Shadow | Custom silhouettes, crawl→stand→attack states, floor movement & drift, particle reveal across cues.
 
 | Communion | Ground ripples, emergence, rise & levitation, a mid-air ripple layer; ~12′ radius.
 
 

 

## Working palette
 
A starting palette for UI, docs, and placeholder material — defer to each scene's own design where they differ.
 
 | 
 | Token | Swatch | Hex | Use
 
 
 | joan/gold | Gold | #d4af6a | Joan's presence, key accents, doc highlights.
 
 | joan/rose | Rose | #c8688a | Emotion, warmth, the human voice.
 
 | joan/lilac | Lilac | #8a7bd8 | The Light scene sky; visions and apparitions.
 
 | joan/cyan | Cyan | #5fb8c4 | Cool accents and atmospheric effects.
 
 | joan/night | Night | #0a0a0f | The dark the AR content reads against.
 
 

 

## Typography
 
Display — Cormorant Garamond. A high-contrast serif for titles, libretto, and any sacred text. It carries the operatic, historical weight.
 
Interface — Inter. Neutral and legible for the rare diegetic labels and all operator/console tooling.
 
Mono — JetBrains Mono. Code, cue ids, asset names, cue sheets.

 

## Light & material principles
 
 
- Light tells the story. A scene's emotion is set by its key light and color before any character appears.
 
- Subtractive design. The real venue is busy — virtual content should be sparse, deliberate, and luminous against the dark.
 
- Glow & audio-reactivity. Characters and particles emit soft light; emissive intensity follows the audio.
 
- Readable on-site. Assets must hold up in the venue's real light — test at the actual performance time and place.
 
 

 

 
 
© Psychojelly · Joan of the City
 
Living document · v0.4

===== PAGE: Brain PC & XREAL Glasses — Joan of the City (brain-pc.html) =====

Brain PC & XREAL Glasses — Joan of the City


- 

- 

- 

- 






 
 

 

 
Engineering
 

## Brain PC & XREAL Glasses
 
How the hardware actually runs the show: each headset's own Android compute runs the Unity build and drives the XREAL glasses, while the operator's “brain” device — a PC or a tablet (TBD) — fires the cues.
 

 

 ✅
 
Grounded in the project. The cue, OSC, XREAL, Android-build and audio-loader details below were verified against the Unity project (joan-of-the-city-xreal); the wireless brain↔headset link is confirmed by the team. The cue fan-out, stem server URL, and PuTTY's role aren't pinned down yet and stay marked (to confirm).
 

 

 
Brain PC
 

## The two halves
 
The show is an Android build that runs on each headset's own compute and renders locally to the XREAL glasses. The brain PC doesn't render — it's the operator's control machine: it runs the cue controller and fires cues to the headsets over a local Wi-Fi network. Audio content is pulled by the headsets from a remote (GitHub) URL, not from the brain.
 

 

🕶️

## Headset (XREAL + Android compute)
An Android build of the show runs on the headset's own compute and renders locally to the glasses. Worn by the audience.
 

🧠

## Brain PC
The operator's control machine — runs the OSC cue controller + proxy and fires cues to every headset over local Wi-Fi. It doesn't render the show or host the audio.
 
 

 

 
Brain PC
 

## How it fits together
 
Cues travel from the brain to the headsets over a local Wi-Fi network. Audio content takes a different path — each headset pulls stems from a remote GitHub URL over the internet. Each headset renders the show itself and drives its own glasses.

 

 

 

🧠Brain PCcue controller + proxy
 
local Wi-Fi →
OSC cues (to all)
 

🤖Headset computeAndroid build of the show
 
USB-C →
render + tracking
 

🕶️XREAL glassesdisplay + head tracking
 
 

 

🎬/cuevisual toggles · Wi-Fi
 

🔊/audio/*stem engine · Wi-Fi
 

☁️stems + CSVfrom GitHub · internet
 
 
 
/cue and /audio/* are cues from the brain over local Wi-Fi; audio stems are pulled from a remote GitHub URL over the internet and cached on each headset.

 

## Per-Joan kit (planned)
 
The working plan is two tablets per Joan plus her group's headsets. All receivers are just OSC listeners on :7000 — the cue tablet targets them by IP.
 
 | 
 | Device | Runs | Role
 
 
 | Cue tablet | The Joan Cues controller app (or the fused server.py) | Operator fires that Joan's cues; targets only her group's IPs.
 
 | Performer tablet | An installed APK (sideloaded) — a browser page can't receive OSC, so the listener must be native. Whether it's the Unity project's Android build (JoanAudio listener), a stripped audio-only Unity build, or a separate native app is (to confirm with the Lead Developer) | The performer hears the show's audio in sync with the cues.
 
 | Headsets (~25) | The Unity XREAL build | The audience experience.
 
 
 
Per-Joan isolation comes from the cue tablet's IP list (sends are unicast per-IP) — save one controller settings file per Joan via Export/Import. Roles need no in-app config: a device's role is simply which app is installed on it.

 
 | 
 | Link | What travels | How
 
 
 | Brain PC → all Headsets | Visual cues (/cue) & audio cues (/audio/*) | OSC over UDP across local Wi-Fi, via proxy.py, to every headset IP in the controller's list (each on :7000)
 
 | GitHub → Headsets | Audio stems + cue CSV | Pulled over the internet from raw.githubusercontent.com/dlobser/opera-audio and cached on each device — no bundled fallback in the shipped config, so first-ever run needs internet
 
 | Headset compute ⇄ Glasses | Rendered frames out; head tracking in | Local to the headset (USB-C); the Android build renders here
 
 | Admin → Brain / Headsets | Management, file moves | PuTTY / PSFTP (role to confirm)
 
 
 

 📡
 
Cue fan-out: the controller keeps a managed list of headset IPs and sends each cue to all of them (one OSC message per headset). The 10.0.1.32 hardcoded on a scene's OSC Manager is just one headset's own receiver address — update it per device and per venue network.
 
 

 

 
Brain PC
 

## What moves where
 
Four flows run through the brain PC during a show.
 

 

🖼️

## Render & tracking
Each headset renders the scene itself; its glasses report head pose locally. The brain isn't in this loop — no frames cross the network.
 

🎬

## Cues
The brain fires cues over OSC across Wi-Fi; each headset's Android build receives them and runs the moment. See OSC control system.
 

☁️

## Audio stem pull
Headsets download audio stems + the cue CSV from a GitHub URL over the internet and cache them locally — fetched on demand unless pre-warmed.
 

🛠️

## Management
Terminal / file access to the brain (and possibly headsets) to start the build, check status, or move files.
 
 

 📥
 
Two separate paths onto a headset. The Unity build is put on each headset manually, one device at a time, while audio stems are pulled from a remote GitHub URL at runtime (over the internet) and cached. Because the build is installed by hand per device, every headset must be on the same version — a missed update means one headset runs an old build and diverges from the rest.
 
 

 

 
Brain PC
 

## What runs where
 
The Android build (Unity 6, URP, XREAL SDK) runs on each headset and listens for two streams of OSC cues sent from the brain over Wi-Fi. Both arrive at UDP :7000 via proxy.py.
 

 🔊
 
Audio stems load via AudioStemLoader, set to Remote in the production scene — it downloads stems from a GitHub URL (raw.githubusercontent.com/dlobser/opera-audio) over the internet and caches them to disk. There is no bundled fallback in the shipped config, so a device's first-ever run needs internet; after that the disk cache makes it offline-safe. Pre-warm that cache before a show to avoid first-play audio lag.
 

 

## Two cue streams
 
 | 
 | Stream | OSC address | What it does
 
 
 | Visual cues | /cue + integer | Toggles a mapped scene element on/off (via SimpleMessageReceiver).
 
 | Audio cues | /audio/cue, /audio/jump, /audio/seek, /audio/vol | Drives the JoanAudio stem engine — play, jump, seek, volume, and StopAll.
 
 

 

## Visual cue map (current build)
 
 | 
 | Cue | Element
 
 
 | 1 | Video quad locked to camera view
 
 | 2 | First Signs — droplets + floor ripple VFX
 
 | 3 | Water splash (31-frame mesh sequence)
 
 | 4 | Joan's crown (SDF particle system)
 
 | 5 | Communion bodies + audio-reactive VFX + BGM
 
 | 6 | PolygonmakerRig 1-2
 
 | 7–8 | Unassigned as of July 2026 — an unmapped slot throws a NullReference if triggered; verify against the current build
 
 
 

 🔊
 
STOP ALL is implemented on the audio side (StemAction.StopAll — "stop all" / "kill all" fades every stem to 0). Visual cues are individual toggles; a single global visual STOP ALL would live in the still-open cue slots.
 
 

 

 
Brain PC
 

## Open questions
 
What the code doesn't answer — the operational and multi-user pieces still to pin down.
 
 
- Internet vs pre-cache — stems come from GitHub, so headsets need internet at first run unless the cache is pre-warmed. Confirm the show-day practice (pre-cache vs live fetch).
 
- How many headsets — target count, each with its own compute on the shared Wi-Fi.
 
- Network — the venue Wi-Fi setup, and whether it has headroom for cue traffic to all headsets at once.
 
- Performer tablet app — it must be a sideloaded APK (browsers can't receive OSC), but which one: the Unity project's Android build, a stripped audio-only Unity build, or a separate native app? And how is it installed/updated? (Ask the Lead Developer.)
 
- PuTTY's role — terminal and/or file sync to the brain/headsets (not evidenced in the repo).
 
- Anchoring on XREAL — plane detection + recenter handlers exist; how anchors hold long-term ties to the anchoring priority.
 
 

 

 
Brain PC
 

## Where this came from
 
The architecture above was read straight from the Unity project, joan-of-the-city-xreal. The source of each fact:
 
 | 
 | Fact | Source in the project
 
 
 | Visual cues / cue map | SimpleMessageReceiver.cs + the scene's OSC Manager (extOSC)
 
 | Audio cues & StopAll | JoanAudio/ — OscCueReceiver, AudioCueConfig, AudioSceneController
 
 | OSC host/port | OSC Manager LocalHost 10.0.1.32, port 7000; proxy.py on :8765
 
 | XREAL stack | Packages/manifest.json — com.xreal.xr, XRI, AR Foundation, XR Hands
 
 | Runs on headset (Android) | ProjectSettings Android player settings + BuildPresets/XReal.preset
 
 | Stems pulled from GitHub | AudioStemManager.prefab / MainScene.unity — BaseUrl = raw.githubusercontent.com/dlobser/opera-audio/main/Stems
 
 | Cue fan-out to all headsets | osc-controller.html — sendOsc() loops the saved IP list; proxy.py sends one OSC per IP
 
 | Audio-reactivity | SpectrumExtractor → AmplitudeFloat_VFXBinder → VFX Graph
 
 | Communion radius | CommunionPlacement.cs (bodies arranged in a circle facing center)
 
 
 

 🔭
 
For the next level of detail — exact inspector values, scene wiring, and per-prefab settings — a Unity MCP connected to the live Editor would let us read it directly. The static project read above covers the architecture; the MCP would cover the runtime configuration.
 
 

 


 
 
© Psychojelly · Joan of the City

===== PAGE: Bridging Dev & Show Design — Joan of the City (bridging.html) =====

Bridging Dev & Show Design — Joan of the City

- 

- 

- 

- 

 
 

 

 
Reference
 

## Bridging Dev & Show Design
 
Engineers and show designers describe the same work in different words. This page is the translation layer — so a "cue," a "scene," or "done" means one thing to everyone on Joan of the City.
 

 

 
Bridge
 

## Why a shared language
 
An AR opera lives where a Unity project meets a theatre production. The same object is a "prefab" to one person and a "prop" to another; "it's done" can mean compiled or it can mean rehearsed and locked.
 
Most friction on a project like this isn't technical or artistic — it's vocabulary. When the two sides use each other's words on purpose, hand-offs get faster and fewer things get lost between the design pass and the stage.
 

 🤝
 
Use this page in stand-ups and reviews. When someone says a loaded word — scene, cue, asset, done — point at the row and say which meaning you mean.
 
 

 

 
Bridge
 

## The translation table
 
Read it both ways: when dev says the left, design hears the middle — and vice versa.
 
 | 
 | Dev / engineering | Show / theatre | What it is in Joan
 
 
 | Cue / event / trigger | Cue | A directed moment fired in order within a scene.
 
 | STOP ALL / kill switch | Blackout / stop / panic | The master safety that halts and resets every running cue.
 
 | Anchor / transform / world space | Blocking / marks / staging | Where virtual content sits in the real room.
 
 | Spawn position | Entrance / mark | Where a character or element appears.
 
 | Prefab / asset / mesh | Prop / set piece / costume | A reusable element — a raven, a silhouette, a particle system.
 
 | Particle system / VFX | Effect / FX / atmospherics | Lilac sky, ripples, libretto particles.
 
 | Animation state machine | Choreography / staging | Crawl → stand → attack; flock → land → fly off.
 
 | Audio stem / mixer | Score / sound design / band | The music, delivered as stems and triggered from the cue sheet.
 
 | Headset client / networking | The house / the audience's view | What each audience member sees, kept consistent across devices.
 
 | Build / deploy | Load-in / tech | Getting the current show onto the hardware on-site.
 
 | QA / test pass | Dry run / dress rehearsal | Running the show before an audience to catch problems.
 
 | Bug / regression | Missed cue / dropped line | Something didn't fire as written.
 
 | Latency / lag | Being late on a cue / off the music | Audio or visuals arriving after the moment they belong to.
 
 | Release candidate / version | Locked / frozen | Signed off — no more changes before the show.
 
 | Branch / PR / review | Draft / notes / sign-off | Proposing a change and getting it approved.
 
 
 

 

 
Bridge
 

## Overloaded words
 
A few words mean different things to each side. These cause the most confusion — say which you mean.
 
 | 
 | Word | To dev | To show design | We use it to mean…
 
 
 | Scene | A Unity .unity file | A part of the show | A part of the show (Light, Voice, Shadow, Communion). For the file, say "Unity scene."
 
 | Cue | A code event / trigger | A stage cue | A directed moment in a scene — same thing, both sides.
 
 | Anchor | A spatial anchor / transform | A blocking mark | A fixed real-world point content is pinned to.
 
 | Asset | A file in the project | A prop / costume / element | Any reusable show element — model, particle, skin, stem.
 
 | Done | Built & merged | Rehearsed & locked | Locked: built, verified on-device, and signed off in rehearsal.
 
 
 

 ⚠️
 
"It works on my machine" is not "it works in the show." A cue is only done when it fires consistently across every headset, on-device — not just in the Editor.
 
 

 

 
Bridge
 

## From idea to opening
 
The dev pipeline and the production timeline are the same path under two names. Lining them up makes planning legible to both sides.
 
 | 
 | Stage | Dev language | Show language
 
 
 | 1 | Spec / requirements | Script & concept
 
 | 2 | Design pass | Blocking & visual design
 
 | 3 | Implementation | Building the scene
 
 | 4 | QA / test | Tech & dress rehearsal
 
 | 5 | Release candidate | Locked / frozen
 
 | 6 | Deploy / ship | Opening night
 
 
 

 

 
Bridge
 

## Hand-offs & sign-off
 
The two points where work crosses the line between disciplines — and who has to say yes.
 

 

## Design → Dev
A scene's visual language, AR interaction, asset requirements, cue structure, and measurements are handed to implementation. The clearer the cue sheet, the cleaner the build.
 

## Dev → Design (rehearsal)
The built scene comes back for tech/dress rehearsal. Design gives notes against the real room, light, and timing; dev folds them in.
 

## Dual sign-off
Anything touching a live scene gets one engineering review and one design review before it's locked (see Contributing).
 
 

 

 
Bridge
 

## Common miscommunications
 
The recurring ones, and how to avoid them.
 
 
- "Scene" ambiguity — clarify Unity scene vs show scene whenever it could go either way.
 
- "Done" mismatch — agree that done = locked: built, on-device, and rehearsed.
 
- Editor ≠ headset — looks and performance must be judged on-device, in the venue's light.
 
- Silent cross-device drift — a cue that works on one headset can differ on another; verify across all.
 
- Anchored vs follow — if content "moves with my head," it's Follow; the target is Anchor.
 
 

 🔗
 
See also: Glossary (project terms), Opera Terminology (art-form terms), and the Cues page.
 
 

 

 
 
© Psychojelly · Joan of the City
 
Living document · v0.5

===== PAGE: Cues — Joan of the City (cues.html) =====

Cues — Joan of the City


- 

- 

- 

- 





 
 

 

 
Engineering & Design
 

## Cues
 
The cue sheet is the heart of the show — for every moment it records what asset plays, what the audience sees in AR, what audio runs, and what the performer is doing.
 

 
 

 
Cues
 

## Overview
 
A cue is a single directed moment in a scene — a light builds, a particle reveal triggers, the raven flock lands, the Shadow stands. The cue sheet is the master list: for each cue it records the asset playing, the AR/visual imagery, the audio, and what the performer is doing.

 

## What a cue captures
 
 | 
 | Field | Meaning
 
 
 | Scene | Which scene the cue belongs to — Light, Voice, Shadow, Communion, etc.
 
 | Cue | The directed moment itself, in order within the scene.
 
 | Asset | What is being played — the model, particle system, skin, or effect.
 
 | AR / Visual imagery | What the audience sees in AR at this cue (from the script's imagery column + Milanote).
 
 | Audio | The stem or sound playing, from the audio cue sheet.
 
 | Performer action | What the live performer is doing during the cue.
 
 | STOP ALL | The master safety — halts and resets every running cue at once.
 
 

 

## Cue types by scene
 

 

💡

## Light
Lilac sky, streaks, chromatic aberration — audio-reactive builds.
 

🐦‍⬛

## Voice
Raven flock & landing, the Hero Raven, collage / sky-text moments.
 

🌑

## Shadow
Crawl → spawn → stand → attack, surround, particle reveal.
 

✨

## Communion
Ground ripples, emergence, rise & levitation, mid-air ripple layer.
 
 

 

 
 

 
Cues
 

## Editing & Cueing System
 
The cue sheet is the single document that ties the show together. It's assembled from the script (the AR / visual imagery column), the scene asset list, the audio cue sheet, and the Milanote reference boards — one row per cue, in performance order. It's the human plan; the technical firing happens over OSC — see The OSC control system below.

 

## The cue sheet
 
Each row is a cue. Keep the row itself short and scannable; put longer direction in a notes field someone can click into for more detail (so the sheet doesn't sprawl). The current working copy lives in a shared spreadsheet — see Tools & links.
 
 | 
 | Scene | Cue | Asset | AR / Visual | Audio | Performer | Notes
 
 
 | Light | 1 | Lilac sky FX | Sky grows from the speaker; streaks build | Radio solo | Enters, faces speaker | Audio-reactive; gradual build
 
 | Voice | 3 | Raven flock + Hero Raven | Flock lands in a semi-circle; one raven approaches Joan | Ravens stem | Holds still as raven nears | Hero Raven separates, looks at Joan, stays after flock leaves
 
 | Shadow | 2 | Shadow silhouette | Crawl → spawn → stand | Shadow drone | Backs away | Particle reveal across the cue
 
 
 

 ✅
 
Anyone can read a well-kept cue sheet and know exactly what plays, what the audience sees, and what the performer does at every moment — without opening Unity.
 

 

## Editing workflow
 

 

## Pull from the sources
Start from the script's AR/visual imagery column, the scene's asset list, the audio cue sheet, and the Milanote boards.
 

## One row per cue
Record asset, AR/visual, audio, and performer action. Keep the row short; push detail into the notes field.
 

## Add performer action
Every cue should say what the live performer is doing — it's part of the score, not an afterthought.
 

## Verify against the build
Step through the cues in the sandbox or on-headset (see Running a Local Test) and confirm each fires as written — and consistently across devices.
 

 

## STOP ALL & running the show
 
At performance time an operator drives the cues from Show-Control. The one control that matters above all is STOP ALL — it halts and resets every running cue instantly. Verify it on the current build before every show.
 

 🔒
 
STOP ALL must be confirmed working before doors open. If it isn't verified on the build you're running, the show is not ready — treat it as a release blocker.
 
 

 

 
Cues
 

## The OSC control system
 
At performance time, cues are fired over OSC (Open Sound Control). The operator drives a small web page of buttons; each press is relayed to the show as an OSC message — including STOP ALL.

 

## How a button becomes a cue
 

 

## Operator clicks a button
In the QLab-style controller — on the Joan Cues tablet app or in a browser served by the PC cue server. One button per cue, plus STOP ALL.
 

## The page calls the local cue server
The page posts the cue to the local server on :8765 — built into the tablet app, or server.py on a PC.
 

## The server sends OSC
It forwards the cue as an OSC/UDP message to every IP in the selected audience (each on port 7000).
 

## The show fires the cue
The OSC target — the Unity show — receives the message and runs the cue.
 

 

## The pieces
 
 | 
 | Component | Role
 
 
 | The controller page | The operator's QLab-style front end (cue lists, audiences/IP groups, settings). Runs in the tablet app or any browser.
 
 | The cue server | Bridges the page to OSC/UDP — built into the Joan Cues app, or server.py on a PC (listens on :8765, also answers clock-sync pings on :9001).
 
 | OSC target | The Unity show on the brain PC — an extOSC receiver on the in-scene OSC Manager, UDP port 7000 (host IP hardcoded to 10.0.1.32, update per venue).
 
 
 
Canonical controller + server files live in the app repo under pc-server/ (psychojelly/joan-cue-controller-android); the copy at Assets/ThirdParty/OSC_Controller/ in the Unity project is the original and will drift until the updated files are folded back in.

 

## Visual cues — /cue
 
The controller sends an integer to the OSC address /cue. SimpleMessageReceiver toggles the mapped scene element on or off — it's a toggle, not a one-shot.
 
 | 
 | Cue | Element
 
 
 | 1 | Video quad locked to camera view
 
 | 2 | First Signs — droplets + floor ripple VFX
 
 | 3 | Water splash (31-frame mesh sequence)
 
 | 4 | Joan's crown (SDF particle system)
 
 | 5 | Communion bodies + audio-reactive VFX + BGM
 
 | 6 | PolygonmakerRig 1-2
 
 | 7–8 | Unassigned (as of July 2026)
 
 
 

 ⚠️
 
This map is a snapshot (July 2026) — the Unity scene changes weekly, so verify it against the current build before a show. Triggering an unmapped slot throws a NullReference: don't fire slots the build hasn't assigned.
 

 

## Audio cues — /audio/*
 
Audio runs on a separate stem engine (JoanAudio) with its own OSC addresses, so music can be cued independently of the visuals.
 
 | 
 | Address | Action
 
 
 | /audio/cue | Trigger an audio cue.
 
 | /audio/jump · /audio/seek | Jump or seek within a stem.
 
 | /audio/vol | Set volume.
 
 | StopAll | “stop all” / “kill all” — fades every active stem to 0. This is the audio-side STOP ALL.
 
 

 

 ⚙️
 
Setup and run steps (Python, python-osc, starting the proxy) live in System Set Up → Running the OSC controller.
 
 

 🔌
 
The buttons do nothing if the cue server isn't running. Before a show, confirm it shows listening on :8765 (on the tablet app: the “Joan cue server running” notification) — and that STOP ALL reaches the targets.
 
 

 

 
Cues
 

## The Joan Cues tablet app
 
A native Android app that puts the whole cue system on a tablet — the show-grade replacement for running proxy.py on a PC. One APK, two roles, chosen at first launch and remembered.

 
 | 
 | Mode | What it does
 
 
 | 🎛 Operator | Hosts the QLab-style controller (the same webpage, unchanged) on an embedded server and fires OSC cues to every target IP — with wake + Wi-Fi locks so Android can't kill it mid-show. Other devices can open http://<tablet-ip>:8765/ as thin clients.
 
 | 🎭 Performer | Listens on :7000 exactly like a headset and plays the show audio — a Kotlin port of the JoanAudio engine (CSV cues, stem download + cache, Loop/OneShot/Stop/StopAll, volume ramps) with a glanceable status board: current cue, active stems, log.
 
 

 
 
- Role memory: the choice is remembered per tablet; switching requires a confirmed tap (SWITCH MODE in Performer, Back in Operator) so a mis-tap can't change a role mid-show.
 
- Per-Joan setup: configure a Joan's IP list/audiences in the controller, Export Settings to a .json, Import on that Joan's tablet — the settings workflow works fully on-device.
 
- Audio sync: the app implements sync phases 0+1 as a test version — Operator stamps scheduled cues (toggleable, off = old behavior), Performer clock-syncs and schedules starts. Validated end-to-end on the emulator (clock offset 0.1 ms; cue scheduled at 1498/1500 ms; 3× dedupe working).
 
- Source & docs: private repo psychojelly/joan-cue-controller-android — its README covers dev setup, build, sideload, and the test plan; AUDIO-SYNC-HANDOFF.md there is the sync implementation handoff.
 
 

 📱
 
Install model: sideloaded APK, same as the headsets — no Play Store. Runs on Android 7.0+ (incl. Fire HD 8 2018+, with the Fire's weaker Wi-Fi noted for show use).
 
 

 

 
Cues
 

## Configuring cues — sender vs receiver
 
Every cue has two sides, configured in two different places. For visual cues you keep them in sync by hand; audio cues are driven by one shared file.

 

## The two sides
 
 | 
 | Side | Where it's set | What it defines
 
 
 | Receiver | Unity — SimpleMessageReceiver, Inspector slots cue_1…cue_8 | What each cue number actually does (which GameObject it toggles).
 
 | Sender | The controller webpage (its own cue list) | Which buttons the operator sees and what number each one sends.
 
 
 
The two are linked only by the cue number — there is no shared file for visual cues. Change one side and you must change the other, or they drift.
 

 ⚠️
 
Visual cues are limited to 8 hardcoded slots in Unity (cue_1–cue_8); adding a 9th means editing the C# script, not just the Inspector. In the current build, controller cue numbers 9–16 drive Shadow animation states (idle, spawn1, taunt, attack1, standup, deathimpact…) via a separate receiver.
 

 

## Audio cues — one shared source
 
Audio (/audio/*) is the exception. Cues are defined in a CSV (audio_cues.csv, hosted on GitHub) that both Unity and the controller read. Edit it once — via csv-editor.html or on GitHub — and both sides update. No manual double-entry.

 

## Publishing a new CSV, and getting every device onto it
 
 
- Publish: commit the edited CSV to the GitHub repo — GitHub is the single source of truth; there is no push-from-device.
 
- Operator: tap ↻ CSV to re-fetch and rebuild the buttons — or use ⟳ CSV → ALL, which reloads locally and tells every device in the selected audience to re-fetch the CSV and preload any new stems, no restarts.
 
- Confirm: each performer board shows a fresh “config: ready”. Do this while devices have internet (they fetch from GitHub) — after that, everyone is cached and offline-safe.
 
 

 📴
 
Offline resilience: the controller caches the last successfully fetched CSV and rebuilds from it if the fetch fails (shown as “offline — using cached CSV from <date>”), and performer devices fall back to their disk cache the same way. A closed show network can no longer lose the cue list to a page reload. Headsets handle the live reload too (implemented 2026-07-15) — new or version-bumped stems load without touching current playback.
 

 

## What the controller saves
 
The controller persists your setup in the browser (localStorage), and you can Export / Import Settings (a JSON file) to back it up or move it to another machine (e.g. the operator tablet). Saved:
 
 
- Cue groupings, group names, and colors
 
- Audiences (which headsets a cue targets) and the target IP list
 
- UDP port, OSC addresses, the audio CSV URL, and the fire-forward delay
 
 

 💾
 
localStorage is per-browser, per-machine. Use Export Settings to save a backup and Import it on other devices — otherwise a fresh machine (or cleared browser data) starts blank. Note the individual cue definitions are code-defined (visual) or CSV-defined (audio); the controller saves your organization and targeting, not the cue list itself.
 

 

## Adding or changing a cue
 
 | 
 | To… | Do this
 
 
 | Re-map a visual cue (1–8) | Reassign the GameObject on the cue_N slot in Unity's Inspector.
 
 | Add a 9th+ visual cue | Edit SimpleMessageReceiver.cs (new slot + handler) and add a matching button in the controller.
 
 | Add / change an audio cue | Edit the CSV (via csv-editor.html) — both sides pick it up.
 
 | Re-organize / label / target cues | Do it in the controller, then Export Settings — persists, no code change.

Design Style Guide — Joan of the City

- 

- 

- 

- 

 
 

 

 
Design
 

## Design Style Guide
 
The shared visual and experiential language for Joan of the City. It keeps every scene, asset, and interface feeling like one world — operatic, restrained, and anchored in real space.
 

 

 
Style Guide
 

## Design principles
 
Six commitments. When a decision is hard, return to these.
 

 

🎭

## Operatic
High contrast, theatrical light, emotional scale. The medium is new; the feeling is opera.
 

➖

## Subtractive
The real venue is busy. Virtual content is sparse, deliberate, and luminous against the dark.
 

📍

## Anchored
Content belongs to a place in the real world — Anchor, not Follow. It never drifts with the head.
 

🎶

## Audio-reactive
Light, particles, and Communion respond to the score. Sound and image move as one.
 

🕊️

## Character, not effect
The Hero Raven, the Shadow, the Communion presence act with intention — never decoration.
 

🛟

## Safe & legible
Comfortable to watch, readable on-site, and always able to STOP ALL to a safe state.
 
 

 

 
Style Guide
 

## Color
 
A working palette for UI, docs, and placeholder material. Each scene may push its own direction (see Assets → Visual language) — defer to the scene where they differ.
 
 | 
 | Token | Swatch | Hex | Use
 
 
 | joan/gold | Gold | #d4af6a | Joan's presence, key accents, highlights.
 
 | joan/rose | Rose | #c8688a | Emotion, warmth, the human voice.
 
 | joan/lilac | Lilac | #8a7bd8 | The Light scene sky; visions & apparitions.
 
 | joan/cyan | Cyan | #5fb8c4 | Cool accents, atmospheric effects.
 
 | joan/night | Night | #0a0a0f | The dark the AR content reads against.
 
 
 

## Per-scene accent
 
 | 
 | Scene | Lead color
 
 
 | Light | Lilac sky, building from the speaker.
 
 | Voice | Black ravens against sky; collage text.
 
 | Shadow | Cool, low-key; silhouettes & particle reveal.
 
 | Communion | Warm emergence; ripples & levitation.
 
 
 

 

 
Style Guide
 

## Typography
 
Three typefaces, three jobs. Used consistently across the docs, decks, and any in-experience text.
 
 | 
 | Role | Typeface | Used for
 
 
 | Display | Cormorant Garamond | Titles, libretto, any sacred / operatic text. Carries the historical weight.
 
 | Interface | Inter | Body copy, labels, UI, console tooling. Neutral and legible.
 
 | Mono | JetBrains Mono | Code, cue ids, asset names, cue sheets.
 
 
 

 🔤
 
Pair a large display serif with quiet sans-serif body. Don't set long passages in the display serif, and don't use mono for anything but data and code.
 
 

 

 
Style Guide
 

## Motion & feel
 
How things move sells the world. Motion should feel composed, not switched on.
 
 
- Gradual evolution. Builds are slow and continuous — sky grows, streaks accumulate, ripples spread. Avoid hard on/off pops.
 
- Audio-reactive. Tie intensity, growth, and glow to the score so visuals breathe with the music.
 
- Intentional entrances & exits. The Hero Raven separates, approaches, and stays; the collage tears off and flies away rather than fading.
 
- Atmosphere over flash. Chromatic aberration, drift, and particle reveals add depth — used as seasoning, not spectacle.
 
 

 

 
Style Guide
 

## Spatial & AR UX
 
Designing for a head-worn, in-the-room experience. Comfort and safety come before spectacle.
 

 ✅
 
Do: keep key action within a comfortable cone of vision; anchor content to stable, textured surfaces; lead attention with light and sound; design for the audience's real standing points; test in the venue's actual light.
 
 

 🚫
 
Don't: head-lock UI or float menus during a scene; force rapid head-turns to follow a beat; lure anyone toward hazards; rely on a single sense for a critical cue; trust how it looks in the Editor over how it looks on-device.
 
 

 

 
Style Guide
 

## Design review checklist
 
Run a scene or asset against this before it's called done.
 
 
- Reads as one world — palette, type, and light match the rest of the show.
 
- Content is anchored and holds its place across devices.
 
- Motion builds gradually and reacts to audio.
 
- Named elements (ravens, presences) behave with intention, not as effects.
 
- Comfortable to watch; no head-locked UI during the scene.
 
- Legible in the venue's real light at performance time.
 
- STOP ALL returns the scene to a safe, neutral state.

How It Works & Recommendations — Joan of the City


- 

- 

- 

- 





 
 

 

 
Overview
 

## How It Works & Recommendations
 
The whole system in plain English — then practical guidance for running it reliably on Wi-Fi: optimizations and monitoring tools. For the deeper detail, see Brain PC & Glasses and System Overview.
 

 

 
In plain English
 

## The whole system, simply
 
Think of it like a stage manager calling cues to a row of tiny self-contained theaters. Nothing is streamed — each headset already has the whole show and just reacts to calls.
 
 
- Each headset runs the show itself. Every pair of XREAL glasses is driven by its own little computer (an Android device) that already has the entire show installed on it.
 
- The “brain” is the control desk. An operator device — a tablet running the Joan Cues app, or a PC running the cue server (which one the show uses is TBD) — shows a page of buttons. Tapping one shouts the cue across the Wi-Fi to every headset at once.
 
- Cues just flip things on and off. When a headset hears a cue, it toggles that piece of the show — the video, the droplets, Joan's crown, the communion bodies.
 
- Music is handled on each headset. Each headset downloads the audio tracks ahead of time (from the internet) and plays them locally, reacting to the same cues.
 
- The glasses just display and track. They show what their computer draws and report where the wearer is looking. They don't compute the show.
 
- Nothing reports back. The brain only shouts cues out — it never hears anything back from the headsets.
 
 

 🎭
 
The mental model: the brain calls the cues; each headset performs the whole show on its own. Keeping them in step is purely a matter of every headset hearing every cue and running the same build.
 
 

 

 
In plain English
 

## Follow one cue
 
What actually happens when the operator presses “Cue 5”:
 

 

## Operator taps a button
In the cue controller — on the operator tablet (Joan Cues app) or a PC.
 

## It goes to a little helper
The page hands the cue to the local cue server (built into the tablet app; server.py on a PC).
 

## The helper shouts it over Wi-Fi
It sends the cue as an OSC message to every headset's address on the network.
 

## Each headset reacts
Every headset toggles the communion bodies on, and its audio responds — all locally, all at once.
 
 

 

 
Recommendations
 

## Running it on Wi-Fi
 
Because cues fly over Wi-Fi as fire-and-forget messages (no delivery guarantee, no acknowledgement), network quality directly determines whether every headset stays in step. Treat the network as show-critical gear.
 

 

📶

## Dedicated network
Run a private router/AP just for the show — never venue or public Wi-Fi. Fewer devices, less congestion, lower jitter.
 

🔌

## Wire what you can
Put the brain laptop on Ethernet to the access point; keep only the headsets on Wi-Fi.
 

📡

## 5/6 GHz, clean channel
Use 5 GHz or Wi-Fi 6, not 2.4 GHz. Scan for a quiet channel; avoid crowded venue spectrum.
 

🏷️

## Fixed IPs
Give each headset a static IP / DHCP reservation so the controller's saved IP list keeps working.
 
 

## Reduce the live network's job
 
 
- Pre-cache all audio stems onto every headset before doors. Stems come from GitHub over the internet by default — pre-warming the cache (or shipping them bundled) means the live network only carries tiny cue packets, and removes the internet dependency mid-show.
 
- Keep the network light — disable auto-updates and background traffic on the headsets and the brain; keep unrelated devices off the network.
 
- Strong, close signal — APs near the audience area, minimal walls between AP and headsets. Packet loss = a missed cue.
 
 

 ⚠️
 
Don't manually “resend cues to be safe.” Visual cues are toggles — sending the same one twice flips it back off. (Audio cues in sync mode are the exception: the system itself sends them 3× with a timestamp and receivers de-duplicate — see the sync design. That safety is built-in; it doesn't make manual re-firing safe.)
 
 

 

 
Recommendations
 

## Performance & optimization
 
Stutter on this rig is almost always local to a headset (it renders on mobile-class Android hardware), and most often it's loading colliding with playing. The fix is to separate load from play.
 

## Separate loading from playback
 
 
- Pre-warm on load: pre-instantiate the FlipBook's 31 meshes, activate heavy VFX once to compile shaders, Prepare() video before Play(), and pre-cache audio stems. Do all loading during a standby phase, not on the cue.
 
- Toggle visibility, not existence where activation causes init spikes — keep objects loaded and hide/show them (trade memory for smoothness).
 
- Use Local / pre-cached audio for cue-critical stems so nothing decodes or downloads at the moment of play.
 
 

## Hold the frame budget
 
 
- Set and enforce particle & texture budgets per scene — VFX Graph + SDF particles are GPU-heavy on the headset.
 
- Bake lighting; keep real-time lights to a minimum.
 
- Target a stable 72–90 fps; if it dips, reduce particle counts, texture sizes, and overdraw before anything else.
 
- Watch thermals — sustained load throttles mobile chips, which reads as gradual “chugging.” The XREAL notification listener surfaces temperature warnings.
 
- Profile on-device — the Editor under-reports XR cost. And keep every headset on the same build version.
 
 

 

 
Recommendations
 

## Monitoring & tools
 
Because nothing reports back to the brain, you have to watch the system from the outside. A few tools per layer:
 
 | 
 | Layer | Tools | What it tells you
 
 
 | Headset performance | Unity Profiler (remote), Unity Frame Debugger | CPU/GPU/memory spikes, draw calls — confirms load-on-play vs steady saturation.
 
 | Android runtime | adb logcat, Perfetto/systrace, Snapdragon Profiler or Arm Streamline (per chipset) | Runtime errors (e.g. NullReference on an unmapped cue), GPU/thermal traces.
 
 | OSC / cues | An OSC monitor (e.g. Protokol), Wireshark | Confirms cue packets are actually sent and arriving; reveals UDP packet loss.
 
 | Network | Router/AP admin page, a Wi-Fi analyzer app | Per-client signal strength, channel congestion, who's hogging the air.
 
 | Audio cache | The project's AudioCacheStatusDisplay | Whether each headset has its stems cached (i.e. pre-warmed) before the show.
 
 | Thermals / battery | XREAL notification listener; device battery/temperature | Early warning of throttling or a headset about to drop.
 
 
 

 🩺
 
The missing piece: there's no health/heartbeat coming back from the headsets, so the operator can't tell if one missed a cue. Two mitigations: keep a human spotter watching the headsets during a run, and consider adding a lightweight heartbeat (each headset periodically logs or pings a dashboard) so drift is visible rather than discovered by the audience.
 
 

 ✅
 
Minimum show-day kit: an OSC monitor open on the brain to confirm cues fire, the router admin page up to watch signal, and the audio cache confirmed warm on every headset before doors.

Documentation

Overview
 

## Story Overview
 
Joan of the City reimagines opera as a spatial, walkable experience. The audience moves through real space while light, voice, shadow, and communion appear around them through XR glasses — Joan's story retold across a sequence of staged scenes.

 

## The core experience
 
Audience members, wearing XR glasses, experience a series of scenes — Light, Voice, Shadow, and Communion — each with its own visual language, audio, and choreography of virtual elements anchored to the real environment. Ravens flock and land; particles carry libretto text; a presence rises and levitates in Communion. The work is performed live, with cues driven scene by scene.

 

 💡
 
North star: the virtual elements must feel anchored and intentional — a raven that separates from the flock, approaches Joan, and acts with apparent compassion reads as a character, not an effect.
 
 

 

 
Overview

Four commitments shape every creative and technical decision.
 

 

📍

## Anchored, not floating
Content holds its place in the real world — Anchor behavior, not Follow. Stable across devices and across a performance.
 

🎶

## Audio-reactive
Light, particles, and Communion respond to the score. The sound and the visuals move together.
 

🎭

## Character, not effect
The Hero Raven, the Shadow, the Communion presence — each behaves with intention, scripted through real animation states.
 

🛟

## Safe & consistent
Cues fire predictably across every headset, and STOP ALL can return the show to a safe state instantly.

===== PAGE: Audio Sync & Debug Mode — Joan of the City (sync-design.html) =====

Audio Sync & Debug Mode — Joan of the City

- 

- 

- 

- 

 
 

 

 
Engineering · Implementation
 

## Audio Sync & Debug Mode Phases 0+1 built
 
Cross-device audio sync — shared clock + scheduled starts, riding the existing OSC path — now implemented and verified in-editor, plus the debug observability layer built on top of it: device reporting, heartbeat roster, in-headset HUD, an audible test tone, and remote mute.
 

 

 ✅
 
Status: Phases 0+1 and debug mode are implemented, verified, and merged to main of the Unity repo (2026-07-15; in-editor against MainScene: scheduled cues land within ~5 ms of a 400 ms lead; the full debug loop reports back to the operator page). Controller/server sides are on main of the cue-controller repo. Source-of-truth docs in that repo: AUDIO-SYNC-HANDOFF.md, DEBUG-MODE.md, and unity-patch/TEST-REPORT-2026-07-15.md. Remaining test: multi-device on real headsets.
 

 

 
Sync
 

## The problem
 
Today a cue means “play the moment the packet happens to arrive.” Every source of arrival wobble becomes an audio wobble.
 
 
- Wi-Fi delivery varies per device (jitter, retries)
 
- Each device's audio pipeline takes a different time to start
 
- A stem not yet preloaded loads at cue time — the big one
 
- Once started, device clocks drift apart over long stems
 
 
Measured side by side: devices land 25 ms to 1 s apart. The ~25 ms floor is jitter + pipeline variance; the 1 s outliers are loading/stalls at cue time. The sync budget is set by the live singer, who performs in real air against what every listener hears: target ±10–20 ms.
 

 

 
Sync
 

## The design — three layers
 
Built in order; each is independently shippable and fully backwards-compatible (a cue without a timestamp behaves exactly as today).
 

 

## Shared clock
Every device pings the master (the operator cue device) NTP-style — offset ≈ ts − (t0+t1)/2, filtered to the lowest-RTT samples — agreeing on “master time” to ~1–5 ms. Runs continuously, so it also absorbs crystal drift and survives Wi-Fi dropouts (offsets don't jump when the link does).
 

## Scheduled start
Cues carry a future timestamp — “play this cue at master time T”, sent ~400 ms ahead. Each device schedules the start on its audio thread (Unity AudioSource.PlayScheduled) — so any packet arriving inside the lead window fires at the identical instant, immune to network jitter and main-thread hitches. Late arrival? Start immediately but seek in by the overshoot, landing mid-phrase in sync. 25 ms floor → ~5 ms.
 

## Position servo rejected as designed
The master broadcasts each stem's expected position; devices self-correct. Rejected for this production (2026-07-15): the stems are short — every cue start already re-anchors all devices sample-accurately, so drift never accumulates — and rate-nudging pitch-shifts sustained sung material audibly. An optional recovery-only variant (a silent device restarts its stem at the right position; playing audio is never touched) stays on the table pending real-headset cue-miss data.
 
 
 | 
 | Problem | Fixed by
 
 
 | Devices start at different moments | Scheduled start
 
 | Start delayed by a main-thread hitch | Scheduled start (audio-thread scheduling)
 
 | Drift during long stems | Position servo
 
 | Device missed the cue / rejoined late | Position servo
 
 | Stem still loading at cue time | Neither — only preload discipline
 
 
 

 

 
Sync
 

## Delivery, mobility & multicast
 
Design decisions at the network layer, and why.
 
 
- Stay unicast; no multicast. Wi-Fi unicast frames get link-layer ACKs and automatic retries; multicast frames get none (sent once, blind, at a low basic rate — and Android filters multicast in firmware by default). This holds under 802.11ax too, where OFDMA effectively parallelizes the per-device sends anyway.
 
- Redundant sends as loss insurance: each scheduled cue is sent 3× at 50 ms spacing; receivers dedupe by (cueId, playAt), which the timestamp makes safe. (Today's visual toggle cues are not idempotent — never blind-resend those.)
 
- Moving headsets: movement changes delay (absorbed by the lead window — size it for the worst route leg, 0.5–1 s) but not the clock offset (a property of the clocks, maintained by the continuous filtered sync). If coverage itself is the issue, the network should travel with the group — a carried Wi-Fi 6 travel router per Joan.
 
 

 

 
Sync
 

## Build order & status
 
 | 
 | Phase | Scope | Status
 
 
 | 0 — Clock sync | MasterClock in Unity + ping responder in the cue servers + tablet app | Implemented ✓ verified
 
 | 1 — Scheduled start | Timestamped cues; audio-thread scheduling; dedupe + 3× sends; late-arrival seek-in | Implemented ✓ verified
 
 | 2 — Position servo | Master show-clock + position broadcast; continuous self-correction on receivers | Rejected as designed — short stems re-anchor at every cue; nudges audible on sung material. Recovery-only variant optional, pending headset test data
 
 | 3 — Ops discipline | Wait for “Audio loaded and cached” on every device; pre-show warm-up of every cue; router per group | Adoptable now
 
 | D — Debug mode | Device→controller return path; logger + roster on the operator page; in-headset HUD; test tone + mute | Implemented ✓ verified
 
 
 

 🧠
 
Master hardware is TBD. The clock master is whatever device runs the cue server — a PC (server.py) or an operator tablet (the Joan Cues app). The protocol is identical either way; which the show uses is an open production decision.
 
 

 ✂️
 
Cut line if time is short: Phases 0+1 alone take the measured 25 ms–1 s spread down to ~±10 ms. Phase 2 adds robustness, not onset accuracy.
 
 

 🔗
 
Related pages: The OSC control system · Brain PC & Glasses · Running it on Wi-Fi.
 
 

 

 
Debug
 

## Debug mode — the return path
 
Until now data flowed one way (controller → devices) and nothing reported back. Debug mode closes the loop: devices report to the cue server on a second port, and the operator page visualizes it live.
 
Devices ──debug reports──▶ cue server (listener)
 │ buffers + orders
Operator page ◀───── live feed ───── server
 
All of it is opt-in and OFF by default — until an operator flips the toggle there are no log hooks and no verbose traffic. One deliberate exception: a low-rate safety heartbeat (one small packet per 5 s per device once a master is known) keeps the roster answering “is headset 3 alive?” even mid-show with everything off — untick Always On Safety Heartbeat on the receiver for strictly-zero traffic. Timestamps are master-clock seconds (the same clock the sync uses), so the controller computes true send→receive margins per device per cue.

 

## What flows
 
Operators broadcast small control toggles (reporting on/off, heartbeat on/off, HUD, test tone, mute, CSV reload); devices reply with an announce, a per-cue receipt with its scheduling margin, a heartbeat carrying clock health and playback state, and rate-limited log lines. The exact message contracts (addresses, argument schemas, ports) are deliberately not published here — they live with the dev team in DEBUG-MODE.md in the cue-controller repo, the source of truth for implementers.

 

## The three surfaces
 

 

## Operator page — 🐞 panel
Toggles for reporting and heartbeat; a device roster (green/yellow/red liveness dot, clock health — clock ✓ Δ0.2ms offset stability or clock ⚠ unsynced — last cue, last-seen age); a CUE ACKS rollup per cue — ✔ cue A_SQ201 → 3/3 received (401/399/404ms), amber until every device reports; a scrolling, color-coded message log with a second delay-graph view (line chart of each device's heartbeat transit delay over the last 90 s, per-device checkboxes to select series, and the log beneath filtered to the selected devices); and the SYNC TEST row (test tone + mute). Every line is stamped in master-clock seconds — sent and reply on one scale: each control message logs a SENT line at the server's send time (and playAt for scheduled sends), then each device's RX reply lands a few ms later, directly comparable.
 

## Unity headsets — HUD
DebugReporter sends the reports; DebugHud draws an in-glasses overlay — device id, master IP, clock sync state + offset, heartbeat status, and the recent cue/log feed with margins. Exists only while debug is on; no Canvas, prefab, or scene changes.
 

## Performer tablets
Already had a status board; now also report back with the same conventions, so headsets and tablets share one roster.
 

 

## Sync test utilities
 
 
- 🔊 Test tone: a procedurally generated triple beep (no audio asset) played outside the cue system — no cue CSV, no stems, no transport state. In sync mode it is scheduled on the master clock exactly like a real cue, so firing it at every device turns your ears into the sync test: one clean beep-beep-beep = in sync; a flam = trouble. Deduped against the 3× sends; shows in the logger as cue TEST-TONE with its margin.
 
- 🔇 Mute / unmute: silences every targeted device via AudioListener.volume — playback continues underneath, so unmuting drops straight back into sync (pausing would drift).
 

 

## Safety by construction
 
 
- Default OFF, broadcast to enable — zero debug traffic during a show unless asked for.
 
- Heartbeat is its own toggle — roster without log spam.
 
- Reuses the MasterClock — no second clock; offsets already meaningful.
 
- Rate-limited and reentrancy-guarded — a log storm can't flood the Wi-Fi or recurse.
 
- Same rollback story as sync: toggle off = the code paths of today.
 
 

 🔗
 
Full contracts and the handoff list live in the cue-controller repo: DEBUG-MODE.md (this feature) and AUDIO-SYNC-HANDOFF.md (the sync design it builds on).

System Overview — Joan of the City


-

Engineering
 

## System Overview
 
How the show is built in Unity: anchoring, cueing & STOP ALL, positioning, and performance — plus the current technical priorities and open questions.
 

 

 
Architecture
 

## Architecture at a glance
 
The show is built in Unity as a set of concerns layered over each scene: anchoring holds content in the real world, cueing drives what happens and when, positioning keeps spawns consistent, and a performance budget keeps it all running on-headset. The whole thing must behave identically across every device in the room.

 

## Tech stack
 
 | 
 | Layer | Technology
 
 
 | Engine | Unity 6 (6000.0.26f1), URP 17.0.3
 
 | XR platform | XREAL (com.xreal.xr)
 
 | XR framework | XR Interaction Toolkit 3.0.9, AR Foundation 6.0.6, XR Hands 1.7.2
 
 | Cueing | extOSC (UDP) ← browser controller + Python proxy
 
 | Audio | BroAudio + the JoanAudio stem engine
 
 | VFX | Visual Effect Graph 17.0.3 (audio-reactive)
 
 

 

 🧱
 
This page documents the project as a set of subsystems and known priorities, not a fixed code architecture. Where a mechanism is still being decided (anchoring strategy, networking), it's flagged in Open questions rather than described as settled.
 
 

 

 
Architecture
 

## The Subsystems
 
Five concerns make up the runtime. Each scene (Light, Voice, Shadow, Communion) is content layered on top of them.
 
 | 
 | Subsystem | Responsibility | Current focus
 
 
 | Anchoring | Pins virtual content to the real world and keeps it there. | Move from Follow → Anchor behavior; a permanent anchoring solution; GUID investigation; image-marker strategy.
 
 | Cueing & Stability | Fires cues (sent over OSC) that drive each scene; the STOP ALL safety. | Debug unpredictable cue behavior (Light / Voice); verify STOP ALL rewiring; cue consistency across devices.
 
 | Positioning | Where content spawns and how it's oriented. | Fix spawn-position and orientation consistency.
 
 | Performance | Keeps the show within frame & memory budget on-headset. | Audio lag, device chugging, a general optimization pass; particle/texture budgets.
 
 | Scene behaviors | Per-scene choreography: Light, Voice (ravens), Shadow, Communion. | Scripted animation states, particle reveals, audio-reactivity (see Technical priorities).
 
 
 

 

 
Architecture
 

## Cueing & STOP ALL
 
Each scene advances through cues that trigger light, particles, ravens, shadow states, and audio. Two things matter above all: cues must fire the same way on every device, and the operator must always be able to halt safely.
 
Cues are fired over OSC from a web-based operator controller through a small proxy — see The OSC control system.
 

## Cue consistency
 
 
- The same cue must produce the same result on every headset in the room — divergence breaks the shared experience.
 
- Known issue under active work: unpredictable cue behavior in Light and Voice.
 
- Cue placement and timing are authored as data — see the Cues page for the cue-sheet model.
 
 

## STOP ALL
 
STOP ALL is the master safety: it halts and resets running cues across the show. Its rewiring and functionality must be verified before every performance — never run an audience without confirming it.
 

 🛟
 
Treat STOP ALL as a release blocker. If it isn't confirmed working on the current build, the show is not ready.
 
 

 

 
Architecture
 

## Current technical priorities
 
The post-workshop priorities, by area. This is the live list driving engineering — keep it in sync with the tracker.
 
 | 
 | Area | Priorities
 
 
 | Anchoring | Anchor vs Follow (must become Anchor); permanent anchoring solution; GUID solution investigation; image-marker strategy.
 
 | Cueing / Stability | Debug Light / Voice cue behavior; verify STOP ALL; cue consistency across devices.
 
 | Positioning | Spawn-position consistency; orientation consistency.
 
 | Shadow | Script movement states; position jumps linked to animation states; refine particle reveal across cues.
 
 | Performance | Audio lag; device performance / chugging; general optimization pass.
 
 | Communion | Debug behavior (with Nina); revise scale / radius / growth; permanent audio-reactivity fix.
 
 
 

 

 
Architecture
 

## Open questions
 
Decisions still in flight. These block or shape downstream work and should be resolved with owners assigned.
 
 
- Anchoring strategy — final approach to permanent anchoring; whether GUIDs solve persistence; the long-term image-marker decision.
 
- Networking requirements — how multiple headsets stay in sync. Substantially addressed by the Audio Sync Design (shared clock, scheduled starts, unicast + redundancy); the remaining piece is the Unity-side implementation.
 
- Device compatibility — target headset count and the brain-PC-per-headset arrangement for the XREAL glasses.
 
- Performance budgets — agreed particle and texture budgets per scene.
 
- Scene measurements — real-world dimensions per scene (captured during setup; see Larger System Set Up).
 
 

 🧭
 
Ready to run it? Head to System Set Up for a local test, then the larger on-site rig.

System Set Up — Joan of the City


-

Engineering
 

## System Set Up
 
Two paths: get the show running on one headset at your desk, then scale up to the full multi-headset on-site rig.
 

 

 
Setup
 

## Prerequisites
 
Install these once before either path below.
 
 | 
 | Requirement | Version / Notes
 
 
 | Unity | 6000.0.26f1 (Unity 6) — pinned exactly in ProjectSettings/ProjectVersion.txt
 
 | Render pipeline | URP (XR-tuned), Linear color space
 
 | XR SDK packages | Resolved automatically on first project open
 
 | Git LFS | Required — audio & model assets are LFS-tracked
 
 | Headset | XREAL glasses driven by their own Android compute (developer mode enabled for sideloading)
 
 | Python | 3.9+ with python-osc — for the OSC cue controller (see Running the OSC controller)
 
 
 

 ⬇️
 
Get the exact editor: install Unity 6000.0.26f1 from the official release page — What's new & installs ↗. Match this build exactly so plugins and XR packages stay consistent across the team.
 
 
# one-time: install LFS, then clone with assets
git lfs install
git clone git@github.com:annehiatt/joan-of-the-city-xreal.git
cd joan-of-the-city-xreal && git lfs pull
 

 

 
 

 
Setup · Path 1
 

## Running a Local Test
 
The fastest way to see a scene working — a single machine, a single headset (or the in-editor simulator), no venue required.

 

 

 

## Open the project in Unity Hub
 
Add the cloned folder and open with Unity 6000.0.26f1. Let packages resolve on first launch.
 
 

 

## Load the sandbox scene
 
Open Assets/Scenes/_Sandbox.unity — a desk-friendly scene that simulates a single scene without leaving the building.
 
 

 

## Play in the Editor (simulator)
 
Press Play with the XR Device Simulator active to walk the scene with mouse + keyboard. No hardware needed.
 
 

 

## Or deploy to a single headset
 
Build & Run to your device, put on the glasses, and trigger the opening cue from the Show-Control console.
 
 

 

## Drive it from Show-Control
 
Step through the cues to confirm light, particles, ravens/shadow, and audio all fire correctly — and that STOP ALL halts everything cleanly.
 
 

 

 🧪
 
The sandbox uses a fixed local anchor instead of a real-world scan, so you can iterate on staging and cues without on-site tracking. Treat its layout as indicative, not final.
 
 

 ⚠️
 
The Editor lies about XR frame cost. Always confirm a scene's performance on-device before calling it done.
 
 

 

 
 

 
Setup · Path 2
 

## Larger System Set Up
 
The full performance configuration: multiple synchronized headsets, a real-world anchor map, an operator running Show-Control, and audio routed for the venue.

 

## Topology
 
One operator station runs Show-Control and drives the cue sequence. Each audience headset runs the client build and stays in sync over the local network. A real-world anchor map (captured during the location scout) localizes every device to the same physical stage. Target headset count is still to be set — see Open questions.
 
 | 
 | Node | Role
 
 
 | Operator Station | Runs the show, Show-Control console, STOP ALL authority.
 
 | Audience Headsets | XREAL glasses + on-board Android compute running the show; receive cues over Wi-Fi, pull audio stems from GitHub (cached), and render locally.
 
 | Anchor Map | Pre-scanned spatial map of each scene's stage; shared to all devices.
 
 | Local Network | Low-latency link carrying cue sync & shared state.
 
 

 

## Bring-up sequence
 

 

## Stage the location
Arrive on-site, confirm the scene's stage matches the scanned anchor map, and re-scan if the environment has changed (moved furniture, new construction, lighting).
 

## Start the operator station
Launch Show-Control and confirm it loads the scene's cue sequence and that STOP ALL is wired.
 

## Localize each headset
Deploy the client build to every headset and run the localization routine until each device resolves the shared anchors. Verify alignment against a known physical reference point.
 

## Verify shared state
Confirm all headsets see a consistent world — content appears in the same place for everyone, and cues fire identically across devices.
 

## Sound check
Walk the route; confirm spatial audio sources sit where they should and attenuate naturally with distance and occlusion.
 

## Dry run the scenes
Operator advances each scene's cues; the team watches for tracking drift, anchor slippage, dropped or inconsistent cues, audio lag, and comfort issues before any audience arrives.
 

 

 📍
 
Tracking budget: real-world venues are hostile to tracking — moving people, changing light, reflective surfaces. Anchor critical content near stable, textured surfaces, and never place a key dramatic moment where tracking is known to drift. This is why anchoring (Anchor, not Follow) is a top priority.
 
 

 🛟
 
Always verify STOP ALL before doors open. Every operator must know how to halt and reset the show to a safe state instantly. See Cues → Editing & Cueing System.
 

 

## Scene measurements to capture
 
Each scene needs real-world dimensions recorded on-site so content places correctly. Capture these during bring-up and file them with the scene's spec.
 
 | 
 | Scene | Measurements needed
 
 
 | Light | Placement measurements, radius, height.
 
 | Voice | Placement measurements, raven distances, landing locations.
 
 | Shadow | Spawn zones, movement ranges, surround radius.
 
 | Communion | Final ~12′ radius, height ranges, audience clearance.
 
 

 

## Pre-show checklist
 
 
- All headsets charged and localized to the shared anchor map.
 
- Operator station on mains power with network confirmed stable.
 
- Cue sequences loaded and verified against the latest scene builds.
 
- Audio levels checked at the real performance time of day.
 
- STOP ALL verified on the current build; safe route and seated/limited-mobility paths confirmed.
 
- OSC controller proxy running and reaching the target (see below).
 
 

 

 

 
Setup · Operating
 

## Running the OSC controller
 
The OSC controller is how cues are fired during a show — the QLab-style web controller backed by a small cue server. See Cues → The OSC control system for how it works; this is how to run it.

 
There are three ways to run it, from simplest to most manual. The canonical files live in the app repo (psychojelly/joan-cue-controller-android, folder pc-server/).

 

## Option A — Operator tablet (the Joan Cues app)
 
Sideload the APK, launch, tap Operator — the controller appears full-screen with the cue server built in; nothing else to install. See The Joan Cues tablet app. Other devices on the show Wi-Fi can also open http://<tablet-ip>:8765/ as thin clients.

 

## Option B — PC, one double-click
 
On a Windows PC with Python 3.9+ installed (tick “Add Python to PATH” during install), double-click start-osc-controller-qlab-version.bat — it installs the one dependency if missing, starts the server, and opens the controller in the browser. Leave the server window open; close it to stop.

 

## Option C — PC, manual
 

 

## One-time
Install Python 3.9+ and run pip install python-osc (Mac: python3 / pip3).
 

## Start the fused server
In the pc-server folder: python server.py — it serves the controller and relays OSC. You should see listening on http://localhost:8765.
 

## Open the controller
Browse to http://localhost:8765/ (or from a tablet on the same network, http://<pc-ip>:8765/).
 

## Target the receivers
Add each receiving device's IP (port 7000) in Settings → IPs & Audiences, select the audience, and fire.
 

 

## Stopping
 
Close the browser tab, then stop the server (close its window, or Ctrl+C in the terminal).

 

## Troubleshooting
 
 | 
 | Symptom | Fix
 
 
 | python is not recognized | Python isn't on PATH. Reinstall and tick Add Python to PATH, then reopen the terminal.
 
 | ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pythonosc' | Run pip install python-osc again in the same terminal.
 
 | Buttons in the page do nothing | The cue server isn't running — confirm its window shows the “listening” message (on the tablet app, check the “Joan cue server running” notification).
 
 | Receiver gets nothing | Check the target IP list and that the receiver is on the same network — and remember Unity's receiver only listens in Play mode.

Opera Terminology — Joan of the City

- 

- 

- 

- 

 
 

 

 
Reference
 

## Opera Terminology
 
A plain-language guide to the vocabulary of opera — so everyone on the team, from engineers to designers, can read the score, the script, and the cue sheet with the same words. Where a term shapes our build, it's noted.
 

 

 
Terminology
 

## Text & Score
 
The written words and music — the source material every scene is built from.
 
 | 
 | Term | Meaning
 
 
 | Libretto | The text of the opera — all the words that are sung (and any spoken lines). Italian for "little book." Written by the librettist.
 
 | Score | The complete written music: every vocal and instrumental part notated together. A vocal score reduces the orchestra to piano under the voices.
 
 | Stem | An individual mixed audio track — one instrument, voice, or layer — kept separate so it can be triggered or balanced on its own.
 
 | Leitmotif / motif | A short, recurring musical idea tied to a character, place, or theme — it returns (often transformed) whenever that thing is present.
 
 | Underscore | Music played underneath action or speech to carry mood, rather than as a number the audience focuses on.
 
 
 

 

 
Terminology
 

## Solo Forms
 
The ways a single character sings — from speech-like delivery that moves the story to the full emotional set-piece.
 
 | 
 | Term | Meaning
 
 
 | Aria | A self-contained solo song for one voice — the emotional high point where a character pauses to express what they feel. The "big number."
 
 | Recitative recitativo | Sung speech that carries dialogue and plot. Rhythmically free, following the patterns of talking. Secco = sparse accompaniment; accompagnato = with orchestra.
 
 | Arioso | A middle ground between recitative and aria — lyrical and melodic, but shorter and less formal than a full aria.
 
 | Cavatina | A short, simple, lyrical aria — often a character's first entrance.
 
 | Cabaletta | The faster, driving final section that caps a two-part aria.
 
 
 

 🎙️
 
Rule of thumb: recitative moves the story, the aria stops to feel it. Knowing which is which helps pace cues and performer action.
 
 

 

 
Terminology
 

## Ensemble & Chorus
 
When more than one voice sings at once.
 
 | 
 | Term | Meaning
 
 
 | Duet / Trio / Quartet | Two, three, or four soloists singing together.
 
 | Ensemble | Several soloists singing simultaneously — often each with their own words and feelings layered at once.
 
 | Chorus | A large group of singers acting as a crowd or collective voice — and the music they sing.
 
 | Finale | The extended closing number of an act, usually gathering soloists and chorus into a big build.
 
 
 

 

 
Terminology
 

## Structure
 
How an opera is divided up, from the orchestral opening to the breaks between sections.
 
 | 
 | Term | Meaning
 
 
 | Overture | The orchestral piece that opens the opera, before any singing — often previewing themes to come.
 
 | Prelude | A shorter orchestral introduction that frequently leads straight into the first scene.
 
 | Act | A major division of the work. Operas are commonly built from two to five acts.
 
 | Scene | A subdivision within an act. (In Joan of the City, "scene" is our core unit — Light, Voice, Shadow, Communion.)
 
 | Interlude / Intermezzo | An instrumental passage between scenes or acts, covering a transition.
 
 | Intermission | The interval — the audience break between acts.
 
 
 

 

 
Terminology
 

## Voices & the Pit
 
The vocal ranges and the people who run the music.
 

## Voice types (high → low)
 
 | 
 | Voice | Meaning
 
 
 | Soprano | The highest female (or treble) voice — frequently the heroine.
 
 | Mezzo-soprano | The middle female voice, warmer than a soprano.
 
 | Contralto (alto) | The lowest female voice.
 
 | Countertenor | The highest male voice, singing in the alto/soprano range.
 
 | Tenor | The highest common male voice — often the romantic lead.
 
 | Baritone | The middle male voice.
 
 | Bass | The lowest male voice — authority figures, elders, villains.
 
 
 

## Related terms
 
 | 
 | Term | Meaning
 
 
 | Coloratura | Rapid, elaborate ornamentation — runs, trills, and leaps — often for a high, agile voice.
 
 | Tessitura | The part of the range where a role mostly sits (distinct from its absolute highest/lowest notes).
 
 | Register | A section of the vocal range with a consistent quality (e.g. chest vs head voice).
 
 | Conductor / Maestro | Leads the musicians and singers, setting tempo and shaping the performance.
 
 | Pit | The lowered area where the orchestra plays, typically in front of and below the stage.
 
 | Surtitles | Translated or transcribed text displayed for the audience (above or beside the stage).
 
 
 

 

 
Terminology
 

## In Joan of the City
 
How these classical terms show up in an opera told through XR. Same vocabulary, new medium.
 

 

📜

## Libretto → particles
The libretto text and data are integrated into particle systems — the words become part of the visuals.
 

🎚️

## Score → stems & cue sheet
The score is delivered as stems, triggered scene by scene from the audio cue sheet.
 

🎭

## Scene → our core unit
Where a classical opera nests scenes inside acts, we build directly in scenes: Light, Voice, Shadow, Communion.
 

🔁

## Leitmotif → behavior
Recurring elements like the Hero Raven function as a visual leitmotif — a motif you see, not only hear.
 
 

 🔗
 
For the project's own production & technical vocabulary (Cue, STOP ALL, Anchor vs Follow, Hero Raven), see the Glossary in Resources.
